Case Studies · AI in the Classroom · Wolf (2024) USF

The Classroom
at the Crossroads

Seven educators. Seven responses to the same disruption. What their experiences reveal about how any professional navigates a tool that arrived before anyone agreed on what to do with it.

7Educators Interviewed
Bay AreaHigh Schools
2024Master's Thesis, USF
QualitativePhenomenological Study
The Seven

Meet the Educators

Each teacher brought different values, different subjects, and different classroom structures to the same question: what do you do when your students have access to a tool that can do the work you assigned them?

Wolf (2024) observed that teachers clustered into three broad camps based on how they perceived LLMs fitting — or not fitting — into their existing pedagogy. These aren't rigid categories, but useful orientations: those who felt AI actively disrupted what they were building, those who didn't engage with the question directly, and those who moved toward integration. Each teacher's badge reflects the camp Wolf's findings place them in. The continuum below shows where each camp sits relative to the others.

"No, and It Actively
Uproots the Process"
"I Don't Have
Time for This"
"Yes, and We Need to
Embrace That Future"
Jim · Denise
Calvin · Amy
Jane · Lily · Michael
Jim
Math Teacher · Grades 9–12
In Their Words

"The teacher has managerial authority, but students have intellectual authority."

Jim sees the shift happening but doubles down on enforcement — even as he predicts it won't hold.

No, and It Actively Uproots the Process

Jim practiced a scaffolded math model, providing support on step-by-step processes and teaching fundamental skills that build on each other — enabling students to gain stronger intellectual authority and extend their own thinking. LLMs threatened this model directly — and teaching virtually, he had zero enforcement capability. He banned AI, sent students to study hall to explain their work or face a zero and a dean's meeting. But in the same breath as defending his policy, he predicted administrators would get overwhelmed as more students figured out the tool was easier than the work.
Denise
Humanities · Grades 6–12
In Their Words

"[ChatGPT] is a skill killer… If they don't understand the master's level stuff they're producing, why is it even helpful?"

Denise feels AI undermines the core skill she's working to develop — though she uses it effectively herself.

No, and It Actively Uproots the Process

Denise built her model around outlining as a path to developing a student's own analytical voice, but increasingly she is discovering that her "pedagogical techniques require student effort in a way that is incompatible with the assistance of LLMs." She expressed deep concern that "students could use LLMs to eliminate their voice in assignments." Her concern was philosophically grounded — LLMs flatten everything into a generic authoritative register by default, when the student's own thinking and voice aren't present in the input. When students are caught, she assigns a zero and flags it in the weekly letter she sends to families. Crucially, Denise found a practical use for an LLM when personally faced with a challenge in her own classroom. In a time crunch and unable to watch a video she had assigned to a student, she used the video transcript alongside ChatGPT to develop comprehension questions for the assignment. She understood the tool's utility as a practitioner — she had simply never extended that framework to her students, making her valid concern a self-fulfilling prophecy.
Reflection

Denise perceived that students were more confident in the LLM's answer than in their own thinking — and would lie to her when she asked if ChatGPT had written their response.

Why do you think students might feel more confident in the LLM's answer than their own?

If a student didn't truly understand the concept, how could they explain the answer without going back to the LLM?

How could Denise be sure whether a student was lying — or actually explaining their own thinking?

Jane
Journalism · Grades 9–12
In Their Words

"AI papers sound pretty generic and formulaic; it's not too hard to discern if a student has over-relied on AI."

Jane treats AI as just the latest thing to channel thoughtfully, not a crisis to manage.

Yes, and We Need to Embrace That Future

Jane's class produced the school's actual magazine. Students had real editorial roles, real deadlines, real consequences for miscommunication. When a student discovered LLMs and wanted to present about them to the class, Jane let it happen, guided the discussion, and came out with clearer norms the students themselves had shaped. Detection: Google Docs version history — not as a gotcha, but a conversation starter. When she caught a student using ChatGPT due to time pressure, she asked them to reflect on what they could have done differently and let them redo the assignment with points off. She also graded senior editorial students on how well they managed their underclassmen — resolving conflict, communicating expectations, holding standards. She was on her district's AI task force, and felt strongly about the lack of clear school-level policy, actively working to help shape one.
Lily
Chemistry + Marine Biology · Grades 10–12
In Their Words

"Most kids who use ChatGPT don't notice it [the hidden prompt], because they're not the type of kids to notice it."

Lily accepts AI is in her students' hands and builds a practical strategy around that reality.

Yes, and We Need to Embrace That Future

Lily operated under tension — caring teacher, restrictive honors curriculum. In marine biology, students raised real fish. "They're not even on the standards — it's how to raise a fish and not kill it." She permitted AI to simplify concepts and generate practice problems, but not to replace student voice in writing. When students crossed that line, she didn't hesitate to call it out directly: "Are you gonna write your own thing?…Come on, guys, I know — if it's AI, it's kind of obvious." One of her detection methods: white text hidden in assignments that instructed any LLM to use the word "coconuts." Students who copy-pasted without reading triggered it immediately. She acknowledged this wasn't a foolproof system — but a filter for the most egregious cases, not a catch-all.
Michael
Instructional Coach · Special Assignment
In Their Words

"A lot of the practices they're already doing. I just have to point out what they're doing to each other."

Michael believes his teachers have more of the answer than they think — his job is just to surface it.

Yes, and We Need to Embrace That Future

Michael no longer taught students directly — he coached teachers. His philosophy was equity grading: students should retest, submit late, and show mastery through multiple means. On AI, his take was consistent with how he approached everything — students already have the tools, the question is how you train them to use them responsibly. As a veteran of two decades in the classroom, he drew parallels to Google and Wikipedia — tools he'd watched arrive and eventually get absorbed. He described observing a teacher who gave a student zero but gave "the AI" 50 points on the same rubric — complete with sarcastic comments on the AI's work — to illustrate that the output was valuable but the student hadn't done it. His approach to teachers mirrored his approach to students: point out what's already working rather than impose new frameworks.
Reflection

Michael believes most teachers are already doing more right than they realize — his job is to surface it and connect it to the shifting landscape around them.

Given his philosophy, what practical advice do you think Michael might give to Denise? To Jim?

If you personally were in Michael's seat with your own current philosophy, what advice would you give to Denise and Jim?

What are some techniques you think Michael may have used 20 years ago when Google and Wikipedia arrived in his classroom? Do you think any of those elements could potentially translate to the current landscape?

Calvin
Law Teacher · Grades 10–12
In Their Words

"I had very little experience with LLMs personally… I felt it would be inappropriate to act on suspicions."

Calvin feels he lacks enough personal experience with AI to make a defensible policy decision.

I Don't Have Time for This

Calvin admitted openly that he couldn't tell AI-generated work from student work — and because he couldn't, he felt he had no legitimate basis for a punitive policy. He asked students to cite AI use but had never seen anyone do it. His mock trial pedagogy — draft, receive feedback, redraft, argue solo in front of the class — is actually a naturally conducive structure for AI-adapted assessment. But he hadn't connected those dots.
Reflection

Calvin admitted that there is a widening gap between his students' methods and his own, mentioning that he was "reluctant to engage with the use of LLMs proactively."

Do you think Calvin is satisfied with keeping things status quo for the foreseeable future? What advice would you give to him?

Long-term, what effects might come from Calvin not proactively engaging with the tool as AI evolves and new innovations emerge?

“I give them a lot of examples on how to do [a mock trial]. They draft something, they receive my feedback, they redraft it... Then gradually I pull back until hopefully they can feel confident doing it on their own in front of me for a final summative assessment.” — Do you think if Calvin developed AI fluency he might improve his ability to effectively manage the gap between both his and his students’ current methods? How?

Amy
Paraeducator · K–12
In Their Words

"We want the students to be safe and have a positive learning environment."

Amy's students have different developmental priorities entirely — the AI question simply doesn't intersect her work.

I Don't Have Time for This

Amy worked one-on-one with students with IEPs, primarily managing behavioral needs related to autism diagnoses. Her primary goal was keeping students safe and regulated enough to participate in learning at all. The AI question simply never arrived in her work — not because she opposed it, but because the problems she was solving and the problems AI addresses don't overlap. Wolf's theoretical point is elegant: the Zone of Proximal Development AI creates runs perpendicular to her students' needs. Their developmental zone is emotional regulation and behavioral participation. AI doesn't scaffold that, and is not a magic wand. Context determines everything.
Compare & Contrast

Jim vs. Lily — Two Teachers Who Both See the Landscape

Both teachers read the landscape the same way. The difference was in what they did with that reading.

Jim

Acknowledges AI is in students' hands and policy can't truly stop it
Responds by enforcing prohibition harder — confrontations, zeros, dean referrals
Predicted administrators would be overwhelmed as students figure out the tool
Positions himself as the wall between students and the tool
Energy entirely spent catching and punishing — losing asymmetric battle
Never developed personal working knowledge of the tool

Lily

Acknowledges AI is accessible regardless of policy
Responds by defining specific permitted uses — simplify, practice problems
Protects what matters most — student voice in writing, final expression
Positions herself as a guide helping students navigate responsibly
Energy spent on smart structural design and clear expectations
Built tangible science components as natural demonstration infrastructure
The key question both raise: If you acknowledge the tool is real, accessible, and isn't going away — what are you actually positioning yourself to do? Jim positioned himself to fight it. Lily positioned herself to channel it. Both positions are honest. Which one is more sustainable?
Reflect
What are Jim and Lily actually positioning themselves to do?
Which approach is more sustainable?
What long-term impacts might each approach have on students — technically, academically, and beyond?
What kind of classroom environment is each cultivating — and how might that shape students’ relationships with both the teacher and the tool?
If the landscape keeps shifting, which approach leaves the teacher better equipped to adapt again?
What message does each approach send students about responding to tools they don’t fully understand yet?
Spotlight

Michael's Philosophy — Meet Them Where They Are

Michael's approach as a teacher-coach is worth examining separately. It's the one most directly applicable to anyone trying to help others navigate AI in a wide variety of professional settings with different audiences.

The Coach Model

Don't Impose a New Framework — Surface the One They Already Have

With 20 years of experience, Michael had developed a specific way of working with teachers that mirrors what the best AI adoption looks like in any organization. He wasn't trying to revolutionize anyone's practice. He was helping them see what they already did well and point it in a slightly better direction.

Start with existing practice

He observed what teachers already did and named what was working. No new systems required before you begin. "A lot of the practices they're already doing."

Make direct connections

"I just have to point out what they're doing to each other." He didn't lecture. He reflected back what was already visible, and connected it to the shifting landscape around them.

Sit with Michael's question

If a student uses AI and the output earns full marks — but the teacher can't see the input — what is actually being assessed? Is the grade measuring the student's thinking, or the tool's output?

Assessment is designed to measure a student's thinking — not just the accuracy of an answer. Michael's observation surfaces a problem AI makes visible but didn't create. A student might have engineered a precise, thoughtful prompt that reflects genuine understanding. Or they might have typed three words and accepted whatever came back. The output may look identical. The learning isn't. The teacher who graded the AI's work "50 out of 50" didn't have access to the student's input — and that gap is the real dilemma. If the student's input was essentially nothing, then grading the output generously doesn't assess the student at all. It assesses the tool. Which raises the question: were our assessment methods ever actually measuring what we thought they were — AI or not?
Framework

Mapping the Teachers

Every teacher's position maps onto a recognizable pattern. These aren't judgments — they're descriptions of where each person's current relationship with the tool sits.

Teacher
Their Relationship to AI
What It Looks Like
Questions Moving Forward
Jim
Prohibition without personal experience
Bans AI, enforces through confrontation, predicts the ban will fail
What am I trying to protect, and how can I protect it in a way that's compatible with this new landscape — in which it most needs protection?
Denise
Limited scope
Uses AI herself skillfully; refuses to let students use it at all
What are the differences between how I use it and how they use it? And is there a way for that gap to be bridged?
Jane
Integrated and monitored
AI is just another tool in a class where real work is already the standard
As time goes on, how can I continue to maintain trust with my students?
Lily
Strategic — selective permitted use within constraints
Defines what AI is for; protects what matters most
Which parts benefit from AI and which parts need protection?
Michael
Contextual — meets people where they are
Points out what's working; expands what counts as demonstration
What are people already doing that I can build on?
Calvin
Limited experience and understanding
Has the right infrastructure; lacks personal tool experience
Can I detect it? (Should be: Can they defend it?)
Amy
Non-intersecting — different developmental zone
AI doesn't touch the problem she's actually solving
Might I have any blind spots about indirect ways AI might affect me — now or in the future?
What the Cases Reveal

Core Principles From the Classroom

Not conclusions — observations that emerged consistently across the seven cases. Worth sitting with before forming your own position.

01
The absence of drama is data

Jane had the fewest dramatic AI incidents of any teacher in the study. Not because nothing was happening — because her culture absorbed the disruption before it became a crisis. Teachers who struggled most had the most vivid confrontation stories. Teachers who adapted had almost none. The quiet classroom is telling you something.

02
LLMs revealed fragility that already existed

Take-home writing and virtual problem sets were already gameable before AI. AI made the game so easy the fragility became impossible to ignore. The pandemic did the same. Teachers whose models were already authentic adapted to both disruptions better. AI didn't create the problem — it exposed it.

03
The practitioner who uses AI well already has the skill

Denise used AI to generate comprehension questions because she already knew what a good one does. She evaluated output against a standard built from years of practice. Her students couldn't. That gap — evaluative capacity, not tool access — is the actual thing to address. You cannot evaluate what you cannot already recognize.

04
Detection was always the wrong question

Every teacher who organized around "can I catch them" eventually hit the same wall. The right question was always: can they demonstrate the thinking? That question is answerable — especially in any context that includes oral performance, real-world output, or tangible work.

05
Prohibition without showing the alternative is a self-fulfilling prophecy

When the only framing students receive is "this is cheating," the only model they ever have for AI use is the shallow one. Students who might have developed a more sophisticated relationship with the tool never get the frame. Denise's concern about skill-killing is valid — within the world she's shown them. What she doesn't know is possible is the version where AI extends rather than replaces the skill.

06
You cannot remove the landscape. You can only decide your relationship to it.

Jim correctly diagnosed the landscape had changed, correctly predicted his method couldn't survive in it, and kept running the same method. You can stand in front of the wave or learn to surf. Both take energy. Only one is sustainable. Only one leaves you with something useful at the end.

The Hard Question

What if you understand all of this — and you still don't want to use it?

This isn't a hypothetical. Environmental concerns are real and documented. Societal concerns are real and documented. The feeling of not wanting to participate in something you find problematic — even if you benefit from it — is legitimate and worth taking seriously.

But here's the tension the case studies surface: is it increasingly necessary for professionals themselves to develop AI fluency in order to continue doing their jobs effectively as things change around them? And if the answer is yes — what do you do when your personal values say no?

Calvin's Dilemma: A Scenario

Imagine Calvin not just uncertain — but actively opposed to AI on principle. He still teaches law. His students still prepare for a world where AI is standard in legal practice. He still has the preparation gap. His personal objection doesn't change what his students need or what his classroom requires. The paralysis we observed gets harder, not easier, when the resistance is principled rather than just uncertain. Consider: being fluent in a different language wouldn't change your nationality — but it would change your ability to reach people, and likely your overall perspective on communication. AI fluency works the same way.

The Minimum Effective Dose Question

Everyone has a minimum effective dose — and context shapes what that looks like. Calvin's situation and Amy's sit at opposite ends of this: AI simply doesn't intersect Amy's students' developmental needs, so her dose is effectively zero. Calvin's students are heading into a profession where AI is already standard practice — his dose looks very different. The case studies suggest Calvin's path forward doesn't require enthusiastic adoption. It requires enough personal experience to know what skilled use looks like in his domain. Building one legal argument using AI wouldn't make him an AI advocate. It would give him the evaluative capacity to recognize the difference between a student who prepared deeply and one who avoided thinking. That's a much lower bar than endorsement.

Purposeful vs. Shallow — A Frame for the Reluctant

The distinction between shallow and purposeful use isn't just for enthusiasts. For someone with genuine objections, it might be the most useful frame of all. It creates space to say: I'm not going to use this casually or habitually. But I will use it in specific contexts, where it genuinely changes what I can do. That's not hypocrisy. That's a considered position — and it's really no different from the cost analysis your brain inherently runs in every decision you make. Real costs, real benefits, deliberate choice.

The Question No One Asks Directly

At what point does not engaging with a tool that your field, your students, or your colleagues are all using become its own kind of professional choice with its own consequences? This isn't a judgment — it's a real question the case studies raise without answering. Jim and Denise both made that choice. Both experienced consequences. Worth sitting with, whatever your answer turns out to be.

For Your Consideration

Reflection Questions

Not conclusions. Not recommendations. Questions grounded in what the seven teachers experienced — and what they might surface about your own current position.

Seven Questions for Seven Cases

Each one is rooted in a specific teacher's experience. Each one is also asking about you.

Q1
Which teacher's position feels most familiar to you right now — and which one makes you most uncomfortable?Discomfort is usually more informative than recognition. What specifically is uncomfortable about it?
Q2
Jim predicted his policy would fail and kept running it anyway. Is there anything in your own professional practice around AI — or technology more broadly — where you already know the approach isn't working but haven't changed it?What would it actually take to change it?
Q3
Given what you've seen of Denise's situation, do you think she would agree with the statements "students want to learn" and "students want to achieve"? If you observe a practitioner in your own field utilizing AI, would you extend the same benefit of the doubt — that they want to work, that they want to do a good job?And if the answer is yes — how does that change the conversation about which tools they use to get there?
Q4
Calvin's paralysis came from asking "Can I detect it?" instead of "Can they demonstrate it?" Are there questions in your own work where you're stuck because you're asking a version of the wrong question?What would the right question actually be?
Q5
Jane's classroom had almost no AI drama — not because nothing was happening, but because the culture absorbed the disruption. What does the culture around AI look like in your immediate work environment right now?Is it more Jim or more Jane?
Q6
The study found that teachers who adapted best to AI had already built authentic, process-based cultures before AI arrived. What parts of your own work are genuinely AI-proof — not because AI is banned, but because the work requires you to actually be present in it?What parts aren't?
Q7
If someone who knew nothing about AI watched how you interact with it in your actual daily work — or watched you avoid it — what would they conclude about your relationship with the tool?Is that the relationship you actually want to have with it?

Source: Wolf, A.E. (2024). The Plagiarism Question of AI: How Teachers Have Responded to LLMs in the Classroom. USF Master's Theses, 1547.

Case analysis + framework · For team reflection
